ocr.text: van Dijk et al. Orphanet Journal of Rare Diseases (2018) 13:92 Page 9 of 16
Table 2 Differential diagnosis of Pontocerebellar Hypoplasia (PCH)
Disease Distinctive features Gene(s) involved references
Congenital disorder of Clinical: neonatal onset multi organ failure, PMM2 [68, 69, 72]
Glycosylation Ia (CDGIa) dysmorphic features, ataxia. Microcephaly.
MRI: global PCH with superimposed atrophy,
supratentorial atrophy, ventriculomegaly,
delayed myelinisation.
CASK- related disorders Clinical: facial dysmorphism, sensorineural CASK [73-76]
hearing loss, ophthalmologic abnormalities.
Developmental progress in a subgroup of
patients. No MND or chorea. Progressive
microcephaly.
MRI: variable degree of PCH, equally affecting
hemispheres and vermis. Cortical malformations
can be present.
Tubulin defects Clinical: DD, seizures. Progressive microcephaly. TUBA1A, TUBB2B, TUBB3, TUBB5, TUBA8 [77-79]
Optic atrophy in some cases.
MRI: cortical malformations (eg lissencephaly,
polymicrogyria) with cerebellar hypoplasia and
brainstem malformations.
RELN & VLDLR mutations Clinical: Severe DD, hypotonia, epilepsy, RELN, VLDLR [80-82]
nystagmus. In VLDLR: non-progressive ataxia,
quadrupedal gait.
MRI: lissencephaly, severe PCH and abnormal
hippocampus, milder in VLDLR.
α- dystroglycan related dystrophies Clinical: severe DD, muscle weakness with POMT1, POMT2, POMGnT1, LARGE, FKTN, [83, 84, 85]
(WWS, MEB, Fukuyama congenital increased CK, ophthalmologic abnormalities. FKRP, ISPD, FKR, FKRP
muscular dystrophy) WWS at the severest end of the spectrum.
MRI: wide spectrum of brain malformations
including cobblestone lissencephaly, PCH,
congenital hydrocephalus.
X-linked Hoyeraal-Hreidarsson Clinical: IUGR, microcephaly, failure to thrive, DKC1 [86-87]
syndrome progressive bone marrow failure, aplastic
anemia, combined immunodeficiency, some
symptoms of DC.
MRI: PCH, delayed myelinisation, focal high
intensities in brainstem and thalamus,
subcortical calcifications.
Pediatric onset Spinocerebellar Clinical: Ataxia, developmental progress, some ITPR1, ATXN7, ATXN2 [88-92]
Ataxia cases with retinitis pigmentosa or cone-rod
dystrophy.
MRI: (ponto) cerebellar hypoplasia/atrophy,
no supratentorial atrophy.
Extreme prematurity (< 32 weeks) Clinical: motor and cognitive impairment n/a [95-97]
of variable degree, autism spectrum disorders
MRI: PCH, signs of cerebellar or cerebral injury,
eg hemorrhage. Non progressive.
MND motor neuron degeneration, DD developmental delay, CK creatine kinase, WWS Walker-Warburg syndrome, MEB Musce Eye Brain disease, IUGR Intrauterine
Growth Retardation, DC dyskeratosis congenita
on clinical severity: Fukuyama congenital muscular X-linked Hoyeraal-Hreidarsson syndrome
dystrophy, muscle-eye-brain disease and Walker-Warburg X-linked Hoyeraal-Hreidarsson syndrome (XL-HHS) is
syndrome (WWS). Main characteristics are muscle characterized by intrauterine growth retardation, micro-
weakness with increased serum CK, delayed motor cephaly, failure to thrive, developmental delay combined
development and severe cognitive impairment and with progressive bone marrow failure, aplastic anemia and
opthalmological abnormalities (e.g. optic nerve atrophy, combined immunodeficiency [85, 86]. Neuroradiologic
congenital glaucoma). Brain malformations include features are (ponto)cerebellar hypoplasia, hypoplastic cor-
dysplastic supratentorial malformations (e.g. cobblestone pus callosum, delayed myelination, focal high intensities
lissencephaly or polymicrogyria) and cerebellar and pontine in the brainstem and thalamus, and subcortical
hypoplasia [82]. Also patients with a normal appearing calcifications [87]. XL-HHS is caused by mutations in the
brain MRI are reported [83]. Isolated cerebellar DKC1 gene (OMIM # 305000). DKC1 is also involved in
involvement occurs rarely [84]. dyskeratosis congenital (DC), a syndrome characterized
